questions.prompt: Your friend is very tired after football. Give advice: "You ___ rest for a while."
questions.explanation: Resting when you are tired is a good idea, so we advise with "should": You should rest. The other options would advise against resting or are not modals of advice.

questions.prompt: Give healthy advice: "You ___ eat too much sugar; it is bad for your teeth."
questions.explanation: Too much sugar is a bad idea, so we advise against it with "shouldn't": You shouldn't eat too much sugar. "Should" and "must" would tell you to do it, and "can" is about ability.

questions.prompt: There is a clear rule in the library. Complete: "You ___ be quiet in here."
questions.explanation: Being quiet is a rule of the library, an obligation, so we use "must": You must be quiet. "Mustn't" would forbid being quiet, and "want"/"like" are not modals of obligation.

questions.prompt: It is dangerous. Complete: "You ___ play with matches!"
questions.explanation: Playing with matches is forbidden because it is dangerous, so we use "mustn't": You mustn't play with matches. "Should"/"must"/"can" would allow or even order it.

questions.prompt: Turn the adjective into an adverb of manner: "The old man walks very ___." (slow)
questions.explanation: To describe HOW he walks, add -ly to the adjective: slow → slowly. "Slow" is the adjective, "slowy" is misspelled, and "slower" is a comparative, not an adverb of manner.

questions.prompt: Complete with a feeling: "I feel ___ when my team wins the match."
questions.explanation: After "feel" we use an adjective: I feel happy. "Happily" is an adverb, "happiness" is a noun, and "happier" is a comparative — none of them can follow "feel" here.
